revision.text: #REDIRECT [[Shinto sects and schools]]

revision.comment: took from [[Tendai]]. This deserves its own article or possibly a deletion, it does not deserve a redirect to a random article, and it has several links to it already
revision.text:
[[File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg|thumb|Hie Taisha, a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei]] [[Tendai]] doctrine allowed Japanese Buddhists to [[Syncretism|reconcile]] Buddhist teachings with the native religious beliefs and practices of Japan (now labeled "[[Shinto]]"). In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>
[[Sannō Shintō]] 山王神道 was a specifically Tendai branch of sryncretic Buddhist-Shinto religious practice, which revered kamis called the Mountain Kings (Sannō) or Sanno Sansei 山王ニ聖 (The Three Sacred [Deities] of Sanno) and was based on [[Hiyoshi Taisha|Hie Taisha]] 日吉大社 a shrine on Mount Hiei.<ref name=":7" /> The [[Togakushi Shrine]] (戸隠神社, ''Togakushi Jinja'') was also associated with the Tendai school before it was separated from Buddhist institutions by the Japanese state during [[Shinbutsu bunri|the separation of Shinto from Buddhism]] in the 19th century.
These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />
== References ==

Sanno Shinto was a syncretic shinto group with elements from [[Tendai]] buddhism.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō {{!}} religion {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sanno-Ichijitsu-Shinto |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref>[[File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg|thumb|Hie Taisha, a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei]] [[Tendai]] doctrine allowed Japanese Buddhists to [[Syncretism|reconcile]] Buddhist teachings with the native religious beliefs and practices of Japan (now labeled "[[Shinto]]"). In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>
[[Sannō Shintō]] 山王神道 was a specifically Tendai branch of sryncretic Buddhist-Shinto religious practice, which revered kamis called the Mountain Kings (Sannō) or Sanno Sansei 山王ニ聖 (The Three Sacred [Deities] of Sanno) and was based on [[Hiyoshi Taisha|Hie Taisha]] 日吉大社 a shrine on Mount Hiei.<ref name=":7" /> The [[Togakushi Shrine]] (戸隠神社, ''Togakushi Jinja'') was also associated with the Tendai school before it was separated from Buddhist institutions by the Japanese state during [[Shinbutsu bunri|the separation of Shinto from Buddhism]] in the 19th century.
These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />
== References ==

{{Short description|Syncretic shinto group}} '''Sannō Shintō''' was a syncretic shinto group with elements from [[Tendai]] buddhism.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō {{!}} religion {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sanno-Ichijitsu-Shinto |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It began in the early [[Edo Period]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Shinkai |first=Sugahara |last2=菅原信海 |date=1996 |title=The Distinctive Features of Sannō Ichijitsu Shinto |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233554 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=23 |issue=1/2 |pages=61–84 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> It no longer exists<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sanno-Ichijitsu Shinto |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/sanno.html |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref>[[File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg|thumb|Hie Taisha, a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei]] [[Tendai]] doctrine allowed Japanese Buddhists to [[Syncretism|reconcile]] Buddhist teachings with the native religious beliefs and practices of Japan (now labeled "[[Shinto]]"). In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>
Sannō Shintō 山王神道 was a specifically Tendai branch of syncretic Buddhist-Shinto religious practice, which revered kamis called the Mountain Kings (Sannō) or Sanno Sansei 山王ニ聖 (The Three Sacred [Deities] of Sanno) and was based on [[Hiyoshi Taisha|Hie Taisha]] 日吉大社 a shrine on Mount Hiei.<ref name=":7" /> The [[Togakushi Shrine]] (戸隠神社, ''Togakushi Jinja'') was also associated with the Tendai school before it was separated from Buddhist institutions by the Japanese state during [[Shinbutsu bunri|the separation of Shinto from Buddhism]] in the 19th century.
These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />
== See also ==
* [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Oyamakui no Kami#Sanno Gongen|Sanno Gongen]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>

This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />

revision.text:
{{Short description|Syncretic shinto group}} '''Sannō Shintō''' was a syncretic shinto group with elements from [[Tendai]] buddhism.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō {{!}} religion {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sanno-Ichijitsu-Shinto |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It began in the early [[Edo Period]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Shinkai |first=Sugahara |last2=菅原信海 |date=1996 |title=The Distinctive Features of Sannō Ichijitsu Shinto |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233554 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=23 |issue=1/2 |pages=61–84 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> It no longer exists<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sanno-Ichijitsu Shinto |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/sanno.html |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref>[[File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg|thumb|Hie Taisha, a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei]] [[Tendai]] doctrine allowed Japanese Buddhists to [[Syncretism|reconcile]] Buddhist teachings with the native religious beliefs and practices of Japan (now labeled "[[Shinto]]"). In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>
Sannō Shintō 山王神道 was a specifically Tendai branch of syncretic Buddhist-Shinto religious practice, which revered kamis called the Mountain Kings (Sannō) or Sanno Sansei 山王ニ聖 (The Three Sacred [Deities] of Sanno) and was based on [[Hiyoshi Taisha|Hie Taisha]] 日吉大社 a shrine on Mount Hiei.<ref name=":7" /> The [[Togakushi Shrine]] (戸隠神社, ''Togakushi Jinja'') was also associated with the Tendai school before it was separated from Buddhist institutions by the Japanese state during [[Shinbutsu bunri|the separation of Shinto from Buddhism]] in the 19th century.
These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />
== See also ==
* [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Oyamakui no Kami#Sanno Gongen|Sanno Gongen]]

In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>

This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />

{{Short description|Syncretic shinto group}} '''Sannō Shintō''' was a syncretic shinto group with elements from [[Tendai]] buddhism.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō {{!}} religion {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sanno-Ichijitsu-Shinto |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It began in the early [[Edo Period]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Shinkai |first=Sugahara |last2=菅原信海 |date=1996 |title=The Distinctive Features of Sannō Ichijitsu Shinto |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233554 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=23 |issue=1/2 |pages=61–84 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> It no longer exists<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sanno-Ichijitsu Shinto |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/sanno.html |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref>[[File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg|thumb|Hie Taisha, a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei]] [[Tendai]] doctrine allowed Japanese Buddhists to [[Syncretism|reconcile]] Buddhist teachings with the native religious beliefs and practices of Japan (now labeled "[[Shinto]]"). In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>

These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />

{{Short description|Syncretic shinto group}} '''Sannō Shintō''' was a syncretic shinto group with elements from [[Tendai]] buddhism.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō {{!}} religion {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sanno-Ichijitsu-Shinto |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It began in the early [[Edo Period]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Shinkai |first=Sugahara |last2=菅原信海 |date=1996 |title=The Distinctive Features of Sannō Ichijitsu Shinto |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233554 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=23 |issue=1/2 |pages=61–84 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> It no longer exists<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sanno-Ichijitsu Shinto |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/sanno.html |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref>[[File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg|thumb|Hie Taisha, a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei]] [[Tendai]] doctrine allowed Japanese Buddhists to [[Syncretism|reconcile]] Buddhist teachings with the native religious beliefs and practices of Japan (now labeled "[[Shinto]]"). In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>
Sannō Shintō 山王神道 was a specifically Tendai branch of syncretic Buddhist-Shinto religious practice, which revered kamis called the Mountain Kings (Sannō) or Sanno Sansei 山王ニ聖 (The Three Sacred [Deities] of Sanno) and was based on [[Hiyoshi Taisha|Hie Taisha]] 日吉大社 a shrine on Mount Hiei.<ref name=":7" /> The [[Togakushi Shrine]] (戸隠神社, ''Togakushi Jinja'') was also associated with the Tendai school before it was separated from Buddhist institutions by the Japanese state during [[Shinbutsu bunri|the separation of Shinto from Buddhism]] in the 19th century.
These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />
== See also ==
* [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Oyamakui no Kami#Sanno Gongen|Sanno Gongen]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>

This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />

{{Short description|Syncretic shinto group}} '''Sannō Shintō''' was a syncretic shinto group with elements from [[Tendai]] buddhism.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō {{!}} religion {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sanno-Ichijitsu-Shinto |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It began in the early [[Edo Period]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Shinkai |first=Sugahara |last2=菅原信海 |date=1996 |title=The Distinctive Features of Sannō Ichijitsu Shinto |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233554 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=23 |issue=1/2 |pages=61–84 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> It no longer exists<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sanno-Ichijitsu Shinto |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/sanno.html |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref>[[File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg|thumb|Hie Taisha, a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei]] [[Tendai]] doctrine allowed Japanese Buddhists to [[Syncretism|reconcile]] Buddhist teachings with the native religious beliefs and practices of Japan (now labeled "[[Shinto]]"). In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>
Sannō Shintō 山王神道 was a specifically Tendai branch of syncretic Buddhist-Shinto religious practice, which revered kamis called the Mountain Kings (Sannō) or Sanno Sansei 山王ニ聖 (The Three Sacred [Deities] of Sanno) and was based on [[Hiyoshi Taisha|Hie Taisha]] 日吉大社 a shrine on Mount Hiei.<ref name=":7" /> The [[Togakushi Shrine]] (戸隠神社, ''Togakushi Jinja'') was also associated with the Tendai school before it was separated from Buddhist institutions by the Japanese state during [[Shinbutsu bunri|the separation of Shinto from Buddhism]] in the 19th century.
These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />
== See also ==
* [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Oyamakui no Kami#Sanno Gongen|Sanno Gongen]]

{{Short description|Syncretic shinto group}} '''Sannō Shintō''' was a syncretic shinto group with elements from [[Tendai]] buddhism.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō {{!}} religion {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sanno-Ichijitsu-Shinto |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It began in the early [[Edo Period]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Shinkai |first=Sugahara |last2=菅原信海 |date=1996 |title=The Distinctive Features of Sannō Ichijitsu Shinto |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233554 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=23 |issue=1/2 |pages=61–84 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> It no longer exists<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sanno-Ichijitsu Shinto |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/sanno.html |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref>[[File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg|thumb|Hie Taisha, a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei]] [[Tendai]] doctrine allowed Japanese Buddhists to [[Syncretism|reconcile]] Buddhist teachings with the native religious beliefs and practices of Japan (now labeled "[[Shinto]]"). In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>
Sannō Shintō 山王神道 was a specifically Tendai branch of syncretic Buddhist-Shinto religious practice, which revered kamis called the Mountain Kings (Sannō) or Sanno Sansei 山王ニ聖 (The Three Sacred [Deities] of Sanno) and was based on [[Hiyoshi Taisha|Hie Taisha]] 日吉大社 a shrine on Mount Hiei.<ref name=":7" /> The [[Togakushi Shrine]] (戸隠神社, ''Togakushi Jinja'') was also associated with the Tendai school before it was separated from Buddhist institutions by the Japanese state during [[Shinbutsu bunri|the separation of Shinto from Buddhism]] in the 19th century.
These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />

revision.text:
{{Short description|Syncretic shinto group}} '''Sannō Shintō''' was a syncretic shinto group with elements from [[Tendai]] buddhism.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō {{!}} religion {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sanno-Ichijitsu-Shinto |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It began in the early [[Edo Period]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Shinkai |first=Sugahara |last2=菅原信海 |date=1996 |title=The Distinctive Features of Sannō Ichijitsu Shinto |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233554 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=23 |issue=1/2 |pages=61–84 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> It no longer exists<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sanno-Ichijitsu Shinto |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/sanno.html |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref>[[File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg|thumb|Hie Taisha, a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei]] [[Tendai]] doctrine allowed Japanese Buddhists to [[Syncretism|reconcile]] Buddhist teachings with the native religious beliefs and practices of Japan (now labeled "[[Shinto]]"). In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>
Sannō Shintō 山王神道 was a specifically Tendai branch of syncretic Buddhist-Shinto religious practice, which revered kamis called the Mountain Kings (Sannō) or Sanno Sansei 山王ニ聖 (The Three Sacred [Deities] of Sanno) and was based on [[Hiyoshi Taisha|Hie Taisha]] 日吉大社 a shrine on Mount Hiei.<ref name=":7" /> The [[Togakushi Shrine]] (戸隠神社, ''Togakushi Jinja'') was also associated with the Tendai school before it was separated from Buddhist institutions by the Japanese state during [[Shinbutsu bunri|the separation of Shinto from Buddhism]] in the 19th century.
These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />
== See also ==
* [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Oyamakui no Kami#Sanno Gongen|Sanno Gongen]]

In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>

This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />

{{Short description|Syncretic shinto group}} '''Sannō Shintō''' was a syncretic shinto group with elements from [[Tendai]] buddhism.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō {{!}} religion {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sanno-Ichijitsu-Shinto |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It began in the early [[Edo Period]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Shinkai |first=Sugahara |last2=菅原信海 |date=1996 |title=The Distinctive Features of Sannō Ichijitsu Shinto |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233554 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=23 |issue=1/2 |pages=61–84 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> It no longer exists<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sanno-Ichijitsu Shinto |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/sanno.html |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref>[[File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg|thumb|Hie Taisha, a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei]] [[Tendai]] doctrine allowed Japanese Buddhists to [[Syncretism|reconcile]] Buddhist teachings with the native religious beliefs and practices of Japan (now labeled "[[Shinto]]"). In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>
Sannō Shintō 山王神道 was a specifically Tendai branch of syncretic Buddhist-Shinto religious practice, which revered kamis called the Mountain Kings (Sannō) or Sanno Sansei 山王ニ聖 (The Three Sacred [Deities] of Sanno) and was based on [[Hiyoshi Taisha|Hie Taisha]] 日吉大社 a shrine on Mount Hiei.<ref name=":7" /> The [[Togakushi Shrine]] (戸隠神社, ''Togakushi Jinja'') was also associated with the Tendai school before it was separated from Buddhist institutions by the Japanese state during [[Shinbutsu bunri|the separation of Shinto from Buddhism]] in the 19th century.
These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />
== See also ==
* [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Oyamakui no Kami#Sanno Gongen|Sanno Gongen]]

{{Short description|Syncretic shinto group}} '''Sannō Shintō''' was a syncretic shinto group with elements from [[Tendai]] buddhism.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō {{!}} religion {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sanno-Ichijitsu-Shinto |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It began in the early [[Edo Period]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Shinkai |first=Sugahara |last2=菅原信海 |date=1996 |title=The Distinctive Features of Sannō Ichijitsu Shinto |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233554 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=23 |issue=1/2 |pages=61–84 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> It no longer exists<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sanno-Ichijitsu Shinto |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/sanno.html |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref>[[File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg|thumb|Hie Taisha, a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei]] [[Tendai]] doctrine allowed Japanese Buddhists to [[Syncretism|reconcile]] Buddhist teachings with the native religious beliefs and practices of Japan (now labeled "[[Shinto]]"). In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>
Sannō Shintō 山王神道 was a specifically Tendai branch of syncretic Buddhist-Shinto religious practice, which revered kamis called the Mountain Kings (Sannō) or Sanno Sansei 山王ニ聖 (The Three Sacred [Deities] of Sanno) and was based on [[Hiyoshi Taisha|Hie Taisha]] 日吉大社 a shrine on Mount Hiei.<ref name=":7" /> The [[Togakushi Shrine]] (戸隠神社, ''Togakushi Jinja'') was also associated with the Tendai school before it was separated from Buddhist institutions by the Japanese state during [[Shinbutsu bunri|the separation of Shinto from Buddhism]] in the 19th century.
These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />

{{Short description|Syncretic shinto group}} '''Sannō Shintō''' was a syncretic shinto group with elements from [[Tendai]] buddhism.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō {{!}} religion {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sanno-Ichijitsu-Shinto |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It began in the early [[Edo Period]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Shinkai |first=Sugahara |last2=菅原信海 |date=1996 |title=The Distinctive Features of Sannō Ichijitsu Shinto |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233554 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=23 |issue=1/2 |pages=61–84 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> It no longer exists<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sanno-Ichijitsu Shinto |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/sanno.html |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref>[[File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg|thumb|Hie Taisha, a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei]] [[Tendai]] doctrine allowed Japanese Buddhists to [[Syncretism|reconcile]] Buddhist teachings with the native religious beliefs and practices of Japan (now labeled "[[Shinto]]"). In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>
Sannō Shintō 山王神道 was a specifically Tendai branch of syncretic Buddhist-Shinto religious practice, which revered kamis called the Mountain Kings (Sannō) or Sanno Sansei 山王ニ聖 (The Three Sacred [Deities] of Sanno) and was based on [[Hiyoshi Taisha|Hie Taisha]] 日吉大社 a shrine on Mount Hiei.<ref name=":7" /> The [[Togakushi Shrine]] (戸隠神社, ''Togakushi Jinja'') was also associated with the Tendai school before it was separated from Buddhist institutions by the Japanese state during [[Shinbutsu bunri|the separation of Shinto from Buddhism]] in the 19th century.
These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />

{{Short description|Syncretic shinto group}} '''Sannō Shintō''' was a syncretic shinto group with elements from [[Tendai]] buddhism.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō {{!}} religion {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sanno-Ichijitsu-Shinto |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It began in the early [[Edo Period]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Shinkai |first=Sugahara |last2=菅原信海 |date=1996 |title=The Distinctive Features of Sannō Ichijitsu Shinto |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233554 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=23 |issue=1/2 |pages=61–84 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> It no longer exists<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sanno-Ichijitsu Shinto |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/sanno.html |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref>[[File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg|thumb|Hie Taisha, a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei]] [[Tendai]] doctrine allowed Japanese Buddhists to [[Syncretism|reconcile]] Buddhist teachings with the native religious beliefs and practices of Japan (now labeled "[[Shinto]]"). In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>
Sannō Shintō 山王神道 was a specifically Tendai branch of syncretic Buddhist-Shinto religious practice, which revered kamis called the Mountain Kings (Sannō) or Sanno Sansei 山王ニ聖 (The Three Sacred [Deities] of Sanno) and was based on [[Hiyoshi Taisha|Hie Taisha]] 日吉大社 a shrine on Mount Hiei.<ref name=":7" /> The [[Togakushi Shrine]] (戸隠神社, ''Togakushi Jinja'') was also associated with the Tendai school before it was separated from Buddhist institutions by the Japanese state during [[Shinbutsu bunri|the separation of Shinto from Buddhism]] in the 19th century.
These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />
== See also ==
* [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Oyamakui no Kami#Sanno Gongen|Sanno Gongen]]

These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Syncretic shinto group}} '''Sannō Shintō''' was a syncretic shinto group with elements from [[Tendai]] buddhism.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō {{!}} religion {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sanno-Ichijitsu-Shinto |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It began in the early [[Edo Period]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Shinkai |first=Sugahara |last2=菅原信海 |date=1996 |title=The Distinctive Features of Sannō Ichijitsu Shinto |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233554 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=23 |issue=1/2 |pages=61–84 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> It no longer exists<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sanno-Ichijitsu Shinto |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/sanno.html |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref>[[File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg|thumb|Hie Taisha, a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei]] [[Tendai]] doctrine allowed Japanese Buddhists to [[Syncretism|reconcile]] Buddhist teachings with the native religious beliefs and practices of Japan (now labeled "[[Shinto]]"). In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>
Sannō Shintō 山王神道 was a specifically Tendai branch of syncretic Buddhist-Shinto religious practice, which revered kamis called the Mountain Kings (Sannō) or Sanno Sansei 山王ニ聖 (The Three Sacred [Deities] of Sanno) and was based on [[Hiyoshi Taisha|Hie Taisha]] 日吉大社 a shrine on Mount Hiei.<ref name=":7" /> The [[Togakushi Shrine]] (戸隠神社, ''Togakushi Jinja'') was also associated with the Tendai school before it was separated from Buddhist institutions by the Japanese state during [[Shinbutsu bunri|the separation of Shinto from Buddhism]] in the 19th century.
These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />
== See also ==
* [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Oyamakui no Kami#Sanno Gongen|Sanno Gongen]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{Short description|Syncretic shinto group}} '''Sannō Shintō''' was a syncretic shinto group with elements from [[Tendai]] buddhism.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō {{!}} religion {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sanno-Ichijitsu-Shinto |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It began in the early [[Edo Period]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Shinkai |first=Sugahara |last2=菅原信海 |date=1996 |title=The Distinctive Features of Sannō Ichijitsu Shinto |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233554 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=23 |issue=1/2 |pages=61–84 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> It no longer exists<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sanno-Ichijitsu Shinto |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/sanno.html |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref>[[File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg|thumb|Hie Taisha, a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei]] [[Tendai]] doctrine allowed Japanese Buddhists to [[Syncretism|reconcile]] Buddhist teachings with the native religious beliefs and practices of Japan (now labeled "[[Shinto]]"). In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>
Sannō Shintō 山王神道 was a specifically Tendai branch of syncretic Buddhist-Shinto religious practice, which revered kamis called the Mountain Kings (Sannō) or Sanno Sansei 山王ニ聖 (The Three Sacred [Deities] of Sanno) and was based on [[Hiyoshi Taisha|Hie Taisha]] 日吉大社 a shrine on Mount Hiei.<ref name=":7" /> The [[Togakushi Shrine]] (戸隠神社, ''Togakushi Jinja'') was also associated with the Tendai school before it was separated from Buddhist institutions by the Japanese state during [[Shinbutsu bunri|the separation of Shinto from Buddhism]] in the 19th century.
These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />

In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[:en:Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>

These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[:en:Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō]][[ja:山王神道]][[ru:Санно-синто]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q4407806]] Wikidata name: Sannō Shintō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Syncretic shinto group}} '''Sannō Shintō''' was a syncretic shinto group with elements from [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] buddhism.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō {{!}} religion {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sanno-Ichijitsu-Shinto |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It began in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Shinkai |first=Sugahara |last2=菅原信海 |date=1996 |title=The Distinctive Features of Sannō Ichijitsu Shinto |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233554 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=23 |issue=1/2 |pages=61–84 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> It no longer exists<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sanno-Ichijitsu Shinto |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/sanno.html |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref>[[File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg|thumb|Hie Taisha, a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei]] [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] doctrine allowed Japanese Buddhists to [[:en:Syncretism|reconcile]] Buddhist teachings with the native religious beliefs and practices of Japan (now labeled "[[Shinto]]"). In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[:en:Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>
Sannō Shintō 山王神道 was a specifically Tendai branch of syncretic Buddhist-Shinto religious practice, which revered kamis called the Mountain Kings (Sannō) or Sanno Sansei 山王ニ聖 (The Three Sacred [Deities] of Sanno) and was based on [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hie Taisha]] 日吉大社 a shrine on Mount Hiei.<ref name=":7" /> The [[Togakushi Shrine]] (戸隠神社, ''Togakushi Jinja'') was also associated with the Tendai school before it was separated from Buddhist institutions by the Japanese state during [[Shinbutsu bunri|the separation of Shinto from Buddhism]] in the 19th century.
These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[:en:Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />
== See also ==
* [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Oyamakui no Kami#Sanno Gongen|Sanno Gongen]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

}}

In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[:en:Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>
Sannō Shintō 山王神道 was a specifically Tendai branch of syncretic Buddhist-Shinto religious practice, which revered kamis called the Mountain Kings (Sannō) or Sanno Sansei 山王ニ聖 (The Three Sacred [Deities] of Sanno) and was based on [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hie Taisha]] 日吉大社 a shrine on Mount Hiei.<ref name=":7" /> The [[Togakushi Shrine]] (戸隠神社, ''Togakushi Jinja'') was also associated with the Tendai school before it was separated from Buddhist institutions by the Japanese state during [[Shinbutsu bunri|the separation of Shinto from Buddhism]] in the 19th century.
These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[:en:Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />
== See also ==
* [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Oyamakui no Kami#Sanno Gongen|Sanno Gongen]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō]][[ja:山王神道]][[ru:Санно-синто]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō]][[ja:山王神道]][[ru:Санно-синто]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q4407806]] Wikidata name: Sannō Shintō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Syncretic shinto group}} '''Sannō Shintō''' was a syncretic shinto group with elements from [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] buddhism.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō {{!}} religion {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sanno-Ichijitsu-Shinto |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It began in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Shinkai |first=Sugahara |last2=菅原信海 |date=1996 |title=The Distinctive Features of Sannō Ichijitsu Shinto |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233554 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=23 |issue=1/2 |pages=61–84 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> It no longer exists<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sanno-Ichijitsu Shinto |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/sanno.html |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref>[[File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg|thumb|Hie Taisha, a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei]] [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] doctrine allowed Japanese Buddhists to [[:en:Syncretism|reconcile]] Buddhist teachings with the native religious beliefs and practices of Japan (now labeled "[[Shinto]]"). In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[:en:Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>

These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [[:en:Upaya|skillful means]] and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō]][[ja:山王神道]][[ru:Санно-синто]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō]][[ja:山王神道]][[ru:Санно-синто]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō]][[ja:山王神道]][[ru:Санно-синто]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q4407806]] Wikidata name: Sannō Shintō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Syncretic shinto group}} '''Sannō Shintō''' was a syncretic shinto group with elements from {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}} buddhism.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō {{!}} religion {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sanno-Ichijitsu-Shinto |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}}</ref> It began in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Shinkai |first=Sugahara |last2=菅原信海 |date=1996 |title=The Distinctive Features of Sannō Ichijitsu Shinto |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233554 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=23 |issue=1/2 |pages=61–84 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> It no longer exists<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sanno-Ichijitsu Shinto |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/sanno.html |access-date=2023-03-07 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref>[[File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg|thumb|Hie Taisha, a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei]] {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}} doctrine allowed Japanese Buddhists to {{ill|Syncretism|en|Syncretism|WD=Q172904|lt=reconcile}} Buddhist teachings with the native religious beliefs and practices of Japan (now labeled "[[Shinto]]"). In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[:en:Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>
Sannō Shintō 山王神道 was a specifically Tendai branch of syncretic Buddhist-Shinto religious practice, which revered kamis called the Mountain Kings (Sannō) or Sanno Sansei 山王ニ聖 (The Three Sacred [Deities] of Sanno) and was based on {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hie Taisha}} 日吉大社 a shrine on Mount Hiei.<ref name=":7" /> The [[Togakushi Shrine]] (戸隠神社, ''Togakushi Jinja'') was also associated with the Tendai school before it was separated from Buddhist institutions by the Japanese state during [[Shinbutsu bunri|the separation of Shinto from Buddhism]] in the 19th century.
These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of {{ill|Upaya|en|Upaya|WD=Q581473|lt=skillful means}} and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha}} * [[Oyamakui no Kami#Sanno Gongen|Sanno Gongen]]

These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of {{ill|Upaya|en|Upaya|WD=Q581473|lt=skillful means}} and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />

Sannō Shintō 山王神道 was a specifically Tendai branch of syncretic Buddhist-Shinto religious practice, which revered kamis called the Mountain Kings (Sannō) or Sanno Sansei 山王ニ聖 (The Three Sacred [Deities] of Sanno) and was based on {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hie Taisha}} 日吉大社 a shrine on Mount Hiei.<ref name=":7" /> The [[Togakushi Shrine]] (戸隠神社, ''Togakushi Jinja'') was also associated with the Tendai school before it was separated from Buddhist institutions by the Japanese state during [[Shinbutsu bunri|the separation of Shinto from Buddhism]] in the 19th century.
These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of {{ill|Upaya|en|Upaya|WD=Q581473|lt=skillful means}} and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha}} * [[Oyamakui no Kami#Sanno Gongen|Sanno Gongen]]

In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [[:en:Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]] of Japanese gods (''[[kami]]''), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.<ref name=":7">Sugahara Shinkai 菅原信海 ''[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2587 The Distinctive Features of Sanno Ichijitsu Shinto.]'' Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 1996 23/1-2.</ref>

These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹), which argued that [[kami]] are simply local manifestations (the ''suijaku'' or "traces") of the Buddhas (''honji,'' "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of {{ill|Upaya|en|Upaya|WD=Q581473|lt=skillful means}} and the [[Trikaya]].<ref name=":7" />
